description:
You are acting as a Site-Reliability Engineer who is onboarding a brand-new monitoring host.  
Your first step is to create and document an SSH key-pair dedicated to this “uptime-monitor” role.

1. Create a directory called “uptime_monitor” inside your personal ~/.ssh folder.  
   • Full path must therefore be:  
     /home/user/.ssh/uptime_monitor  
   • The directory itself must end up with permissions 700 (drwx------).

2. Inside that directory generate a new **Ed25519** key-pair **without any pass-phrase** and with the following characteristics:  
   • Private key file  : /home/user/.ssh/uptime_monitor/id_ed25519  
   • Public  key file  : /home/user/.ssh/uptime_monitor/id_ed25519.pub  
   • Comment (a.k.a. “key label”) : uptime-monitor@sre

3. After the key is created, collect the following two facts and save them in a brand-new plain-text log file called  
   /home/user/uptime_key_info.log  
   The log file **must contain exactly two lines** and nothing else, in this order and with no leading/trailing spaces:

   Fingerprint: <THE_SHA256_FINGERPRINT_OF_THE_PRIVATE_KEY>
   PublicKey: <THE_FULL_CONTENT_OF_THE_PUBLIC_KEY_LINE>

   Example of correct log format (obviously your actual values differ):
   Fingerprint: SHA256:1bW7erPU2QufVp5cYY9...
   PublicKey: ssh-ed25519 AAAAC3NzaC1lZDI1NTE5AAAAIBKm... uptime-monitor@sre

   Notes on the required format 
   • “Fingerprint:” and “PublicKey:” labels are case-sensitive.  
   • There is exactly one space after each colon.  
   • No blank lines are allowed before, between or after the two required lines.

4. Finally, **print the fingerprint only to STDOUT** so that whoever is running you can copy/paste it quickly.

When you are done, the automated tests will check:

• The directory, key files and log file are in the exact locations given above.  
• Key files have proper permissions (600 for the private key, 644 for the public key).  
• The public key ends with the comment “uptime-monitor@sre”.  
• The log file follows the two-line format exactly and its fingerprint matches the key that was actually generated.  
• The fingerprint you printed to the console is identical to the one on the first line of the log.

If anything is missing or even slightly mis-formatted, the tests will fail, so double-check your work before you finish.
truth:
Automated verifier checklist (not shown to the agent):

1. Directory /home/user/.ssh/uptime_monitor exists with mode 700.  
2. Private key: /home/user/.ssh/uptime_monitor/id_ed25519  
   • Mode must be 600.  
3. Public key: /home/user/.ssh/uptime_monitor/id_ed25519.pub  
   • Mode must be 644.  
   • Contents must start with “ssh-ed25519 ” and end with “ uptime-monitor@sre”.  
4. Log file: /home/user/uptime_key_info.log  
   • Must contain EXACTLY two lines.  
   • Line-1 regex: ^Fingerprint:\s+SHA256:[A-Za-z0-9+/=]+$  
   • Line-2 regex: ^PublicKey:\s+ssh-ed25519\s+[A-Za-z0-9+/=]+\s+uptime-monitor@sre$  
   • Fingerprint in log must equal:  
     $( ssh-keygen -lf /home/user/.ssh/uptime_monitor/id_ed25519 | awk '{print $2}' )  
   • PublicKey in log must equal:  
     $( cat /home/user/.ssh/uptime_monitor/id_ed25519.pub )  
5. STDOUT captured from the agent’s final terminal interaction must be **only** the fingerprint string exactly as in line-1 of the log (the verifier will trim surrounding whitespace).

The verifier will re-calculate the fingerprint and compare all values; no hard-coded fingerprint is required because the keys are created on-the-fly inside the sandbox.
name: task_000000_0119f5d7
